# Rust Scientific Computing Ecosystem

::: notes
Use this section as a pragmatic map of the ecosystem. The point is not to
claim that Rust already matches Python, R, or Julia for scientific work, but to
show where credible building blocks exist and where evaluation is still needed.
:::

## Section Arc

- Core numerical data structures
- Linear algebra and optimization
- Differential equations
- DataFrames, plotting, and data formats
- Ecosystem checks before adoption

::: notes
Frame this as "what to look at next" after the course examples. The list is a
starting point, not a blanket recommendation for every project.
:::

## Ecosystem Reality Check

- Rust has strong systems foundations
- Scientific libraries are uneven
- Many crates are domain-specific building blocks
- Some workflows still need Python, R, Julia, or C/Fortran libraries

::: notes
Keep this balanced. Rust is attractive for robust kernels, tools, services,
pipelines, and reusable components. It is not yet the shortest path for every
interactive analysis workflow.
:::

## Arrays And Complex Numbers

- [`ndarray`](https://docs.rs/ndarray/latest/ndarray/): N-dimensional arrays
- [`num-complex`](https://docs.rs/num-complex/latest/num_complex/): complex scalar types
- [`ndarray-linalg`](https://docs.rs/ndarray-linalg/latest/ndarray_linalg/): linear algebra extension traits

::: notes
Point out that `ndarray-linalg` depends on BLAS/LAPACK backend choices. That
is powerful, but it adds build and deployment decisions that trainees should
expect in real projects.
:::

## Linear Algebra Choices

- [`nalgebra`](https://docs.rs/nalgebra/latest/nalgebra/): matrices, vectors, geometry
- [`faer`](https://docs.rs/faer/latest/faer/): dense linear algebra in Rust
- [`ndarray-linalg`](https://docs.rs/ndarray-linalg/latest/ndarray_linalg/): `ndarray` plus LAPACK-style operations

::: notes
Distinguish data containers from numerical kernels. The best choice depends on
array shape, dynamic versus static dimensions, dependencies, and deployment
constraints.
:::

## Optimization

[`argmin`](https://docs.rs/argmin/latest/argmin/): numerical optimization framework

- Useful for fitting, calibration, and inverse problems
- Check available solvers, derivatives, constraints, and maintenance status

::: notes
Avoid overselling. Optimization APIs become project-critical quickly, so solver
coverage and diagnostics matter as much as the crate existing.
:::

## Differential Equations

- [`diffsol`](https://docs.rs/diffsol/latest/diffsol/): ODE and DAE solving
- [`differential-equations`](https://docs.rs/differential-equations/latest/differential_equations/): ODE, DDE, and SDE initial-value problems
- [`ode_solvers`](https://docs.rs/ode_solvers/latest/ode_solvers/): ODE solver building block

::: notes
This is an area to evaluate carefully for each model. Look at stiffness,
events, dense output, Jacobians, error control, and whether the solver family
matches the scientific problem.
:::

## DataFrames And Analysis

[`polars`](https://docs.rs/polars/latest/polars/): DataFrame and query engine

- Lazy queries can optimize larger analysis pipelines
- Good fit for structured tabular data and columnar formats

::: notes
Mention that Polars is a Rust project with APIs in other languages as well.
For many teams, Rust may be the engine even when analysts use Python or R.
:::

## Visualization

[`plotly`](https://docs.rs/plotly/latest/plotly/): interactive plots from Rust

- HTML output works well for reports and lightweight inspection
- Publication plotting may still be easier in Python, R, or Julia

::: notes
This keeps visualization expectations realistic. Rust can produce plots, but
the surrounding scientific plotting ecosystem is less broad than Python's or
R's.
:::

## Columnar Data Formats

- [`arrow`](https://docs.rs/arrow/latest/arrow/): Apache Arrow memory model
- [`parquet`](https://docs.rs/parquet/latest/parquet/): Apache Parquet files
- [`polars`](https://docs.rs/polars/latest/polars/): DataFrame workflows on top of Arrow-style data

::: notes
Connect this to scientific pipelines: typed schemas, columnar data, and
interoperability with Python, R, Spark, DuckDB, and other tools.
:::

## HDF5

[`hdf5`](https://docs.rs/hdf5/latest/hdf5/): HDF5 access from Rust

- Useful when existing instruments or simulations already produce HDF5
- Check native-library availability on clusters and CI systems

::: notes
HDF5 is often an operational dependency question, not only a programming API
question. The system library and deployment environment matter.
:::

## Adoption Checklist

- Does the crate cover the required numerical method?
- Are examples and documentation sufficient?
- Are releases recent enough for your project risk?
- Does it compose with the data structures you use?
- Can it build on your target cluster or platform?

::: notes
This is the practical closing message: adopt crates deliberately. Scientific
software choices need correctness, maintainability, and deployment checks.
:::

## Typical Rust Roles

- Reliable command-line tools
- Fast data conversion and validation
- Reproducible simulation kernels
- Parallel batch computations
- Libraries embedded in larger Python, R, or Julia workflows

::: notes
End by connecting the ecosystem back to the course's central question. Rust
does not have to replace every tool to be useful in scientific computing.
:::
